summary: The Gemara analyzes bar Kappara's citation of R. Meir about wood added from the sides of the altar, discusses whether that wood is valid for the altar fire, and then addresses which mitzvot override Shabbat and which override the prohibition of offering in a state of ritual impurity.

display.whats: What does bar Kappara teach in R. Meir's name, and does the wood added from the sides of the altar count as a valid arrangement?
display.hint: Bar Kappara taught: R. Meir would say that wood added from the sides of the altar (not from the main arrangements) is not counted as a distinct arrangement. The Gemara then pivots to the question of which prohibitions are overridden by the Temple service and which are not.
display.title: Wood From the Sides Doesn't Count, and Who Overrides Shabbat

learning.ahaMoment: Communal public offerings override Shabbat and communal tumah. Individual offerings and certain other prohibitions are not overridden. The distinction is between 'tzibur' (communal) and 'yachid' (individual).
learning.memoryAnchor: Bar Kappara teaching R. Meir: wood from the sides of the altar is not a valid arrangement.
learning.learnerQuestion: Does wood placed on the sides of the altar count as a valid arrangement, and which Temple services are permitted to override Shabbat and ritual impurity?
learning.coreTension: Bar Kappara cites R. Meir that wood from the altar's sides is not counted as a valid arrangement, and the Gemara must explain what this teaches beyond what was already known, before turning to the broader question of which prohibitions communal Temple service may override.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara clarifies that wood placed on the sides (not the designated arrangement positions) does not count, and establishes the broader principle that communal public offerings override Shabbat and communal ritual impurity, while individual offerings do not.

takeaway.text: Wood placed on the sides of the altar, rather than the designated arrangement positions, is not counted as a valid arrangement; communal public offerings override Shabbat and ritual impurity, but individual offerings do not.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume the override of Shabbat and impurity applies to any Temple offering, missing that the Gemara restricts it specifically to communal (tzibur) offerings, not individual (yachid) ones.

misconceptions.misconception: Any offering brought in the Temple, communal or individual, overrides Shabbat and ritual impurity restrictions.
misconceptions.correction: The override applies specifically to communal public offerings, such as the tamid and musaf - individual offerings do not override Shabbat or ritual impurity.

visualizableElements.label: communal offerings override Shabbat/tumah vs private offerings do not
sides of altar - not valid positions for wood arrangement

quizSeeds.answer: No. Only communal public offerings (like the tamid and musaf) override Shabbat and communal ritual impurity.
quizSeeds.question: Do private offerings override Shabbat in the Temple?

sugyot.finalRuling: Wood from the sides of the altar is not a valid arrangement; communal offerings override Shabbat and tumah; private offerings do not.

glossary.en: Communal, public - communal offerings override Shabbat and ritual impurity; individual offerings do not.
Ritual impurity - certain forms are overridden by communal Temple service needs.

rashiTranslations.en: Wood added from the sides (not from the main arrangement positions) - bar Kappara quotes R. Meir.
The sides of the altar are the north and south faces - not the designated arrangement areas.
R. Meir: wood placed on these sides doesn't count as one of the four (or five) formal arrangements.
What new information does bar Kappara provide by saying 'R. Meir would say'?
The point is that R. Meir himself explicitly said this - it is not just inferred from his position.
Bar Kappara was a student of R. Yehuda HaNasi who transmitted traditions in exact attribution.
The Gemara: R. Meir's ruling that wood from the sides is invalid - why exactly?
Because the Torah says 'the priest shall arrange wood on the fire' - 'on the fire' means on the main fire.
The fire that is 'on the altar' in the prescribed location is the one that matters.
Wood placed on the sides does not connect to the main arrangement fire.
Transition to the broader question: what overrides what in Temple service?
The principle: communal public offerings override Shabbat because 'it is the Shabbat of God in your settlements' excludes Temple service.
'In your settlements' is read as limiting Shabbat to outside the Temple.
Ritual impurity of the community: if the majority of Israel is impure, the offering proceeds.
This is called 'tumah hutrah b'tzibur' - impurity is permitted when the community is the agent.
But individual ritual impurity does not override - a private person must wait until pure.
The rationale: communal needs take precedence; private purity concerns do not.
Rambam codifies this: public offerings may be brought in tumah; private offerings may not.
This principle explains many Temple service edge cases throughout the Talmud.
The Gemara will now test this principle against various scenarios.
What happens when a majority of kohanim (not the community) are impure - does the offering proceed?
Some hold that kohen-impurity follows the community rule; others hold it is stricter.
The rule 'tumah hutrah b'tzibur' applies when the offering itself is communal.
The perpetual fire prohibition and overriding it for Shabbat - this is a separate category.
The altar fire must be maintained on Shabbat - it is not extinguished for Shabbat.
This is not 'overriding' Shabbat - the fire was never included in the Shabbat prohibition.
The distinction between 'hutrah' (permitted) and 'dechuyah' (pushed aside) for tumah in Temple.
'Hutrah' means never prohibited in this context; 'dechuyah' means prohibited but suspended.
This distinction has practical implications for double-impurity or late-arrival scenarios.
The Gemara resolves that tumah in public is hutrah (permitted), not merely dechuyah.
46a is a transition page from wood arrangements to the broader overriding principles.
The next page continues with the perpetual fire law and its implications.
